# Transcrição: 14-Closing Financials

- **Fonte:** 14-Closing Financials
- **Duração:** 03:36
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

Welcome to the last lesson in week four. In the previous lesson we talked about what you can expect on closing day. Some of the final due diligence that you and your team will be handling and how you can potentially chip away at some of those closing costs. So this lesson is covering what happens after the documents are signed and the closing is over and the business is yours. We're going to focus on where the money flows so you understand the process. The morning of or the day before closing day your lawyer will put the money in escrow ready to be transferred. Once the paperwork is signed and the deal is closed the funds are transferred. The lawyers will take their fees and the remaining money will hit the seller's business account. Then you take your cash and fees and pay your contingent fee lawyer and anyone else who is working with you on the contingency basis. And then finally the remaining cash goes back into the business. You might even pay yourself a small amount of money but the majority is going to go back into the business for its growth marketing hiring etc. If you don't yet have access to the business bank account then we will need to get you access. You need to be able to access any surplus cash that your deal generated and mainly for a total control of the bank account going forward. In fact I would recommend you set up your own bank account. We cover the bank account transferring and other similar stuff in more detail in the next module called life after acquisition. But online banking is really important to make sure the passwords and accesses are changed. Anything the business accesses online that the financial transactions are involved with needs to be changed. You could have thousands of customers trying to pay you and their payment not going through. That could cause a huge problem. And if your deal has generated any surplus cash now is the time for you to go in and access it. You might need to obtain access personally as the new owner. Bank changes can be made by the lawyer at closing as part of the ancillary documents that need to get signed. But if that doesn't happen for whatever reason you can take your purchase contract to the bank and get them to make those changes. And you'll want to make sure that you do that immediately as soon as the bank opens the next day. You'll put yourself on the register of the signatures and remove the seller and anyone else if they're not staying on as a co-owner alongside you or somebody who's going to be responsible for signing checks. Any outstanding checkbook should also be tracked down as well as company credit or debit cards. And if you can't find them all call the bank and cancel them immediately. While you're doing that it is also

## [03:01 - 03:36]

a good time to review the company's expense policy and make any necessary changes. A lot of time these types of businesses were targeting or having very generous policies and this is a quick way to start cutting costs. Your business might not even need an expense policy at all. If you do though it's a good place to start with a stringent one. And that's it for this lesson. I know this is a short lesson but I wanted to make sure that you fully understand the process. I'll see you soon on the next lesson.
